result.markdown:
---
title: "5 · Predicting fraud"
subtitle: "From behavioural signals to flag probabilities — read with a fraud analyst's skepticism"
---

Chapter 3 established that *which* token dominates an address's inflow is
associated with its fraud status. This chapter turns from association to
conditional prediction: **can transaction behaviour — how much an address
transacts, how much of that is token activity, and how quickly it sends — predict
the likelihood of a fraud flag? And does the token ecosystem change what those
behaviours mean?** The goal is inferential understanding of the signals, not a
deployable classifier; that distinction ends up mattering more here than in any
other chapter.

## The analysis sample — and what the missing data quietly revealed


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(broom)
library(car)
library(pROC)

trxs <- readRDS("data/eth_clean.rds")

lr <- trxs %>%
  filter(!is.na(total_erc20_tnxs)) %>%
  add_count(erc20_most_rec_token_type, name = "type_n") %>%
  mutate(
    token_rec  = ifelse(type_n < 40, "Low Count", erc20_most_rec_token_type),
    token_rec  = relevel(factor(token_rec), ref = "None"),
    tnx_log    = log10(total_transactions + 1),
    erc20_log  = log10(total_erc20_tnxs + 1),
    avgmin_log = log10(avg_min_between_sent_tnx + 1)
  )

n_excl  <- sum(is.na(trxs$total_erc20_tnxs))
excl_fr <- sum(trxs$flag[is.na(trxs$total_erc20_tnxs)])
cat(nrow(lr), "addresses in sample;", sum(lr$flag), "flagged",
    sprintf("(%.1f%%; majority-class baseline accuracy %.1f%%)",
            100 * mean(lr$flag), 100 * (1 - mean(lr$flag))))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
9012 addresses in sample; 1350 flagged (15.0%; majority-class baseline accuracy 85.0%)
```


:::
:::


The model needs the ERC20 aggregates, so the 829 addresses lacking them are
excluded — and that routine step surfaced something worth pausing on. The fraud
share drops from 22.1% to 15.0%, which is a
large move for a 8% exclusion, and the reason is stark: **all 829 of the
excluded addresses are flagged fraudulent** (829/829). In other
words, in this dataset "ERC20 data missing" perfectly predicts the label. The most
plausible cause is mechanical — the fraud-address list appears to have been
assembled separately and joined in, with the ERC20 aggregates never computed for
part of that batch — which makes missingness an artefact of dataset construction,
not a behaviour of fraudsters. The practical consequences: missingness must never
be used as a feature on this data, and everything below speaks for token-active
addresses only. Low-frequency token types (< 40 addresses) are pooled into a
`"Low Count"` level so group estimates stay stable, and `"None"` — no dominant
ERC20 inflow — is kept as the meaningful reference category.

## Model 1: what behaviour alone says


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
m1 <- glm(flag ~ tnx_log + erc20_log + avgmin_log,
          family = binomial, data = lr)

tidy(m1, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Model 1 — odds ratios with 95% CIs. Each OR is the multiplicative change in fraud odds for a ×10 change in the predictor.")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Model 1 — odds ratios with 95% CIs. Each OR is the multiplicative change in fraud odds for a ×10 change in the predictor.

|term        | estimate| std.error| statistic| p.value| conf.low| conf.high|
|:-----------|--------:|---------:|---------:|-------:|--------:|---------:|
|(Intercept) |    0.675|     0.060|    -6.528|       0|    0.600|     0.760|
|tnx_log     |    0.204|     0.070|   -22.820|       0|    0.178|     0.234|
|erc20_log   |    4.601|     0.070|    21.925|       0|    4.016|     5.277|
|avgmin_log  |    0.835|     0.022|    -8.165|       0|    0.799|     0.872|


:::
:::




Read on the ×10 scale, the three effects sketch a coherent behavioural
fingerprint. A ×10 increase in **ordinary transactions** multiplies the fraud odds
by 0.2 — sustained, plain on-chain activity is what
legitimacy looks like. A ×10 increase in **ERC20 token transactions** multiplies
the odds by 4.6 — the flagged population is
token-transfer-heavy. And a ×10 *longer* **average gap between sent transactions**
multiplies the odds by 0.83: quicker sending rhythms
lean fraudulent, echoing the cadence difference found in Chapter 3. Fraud, in this
data, looks token-heavy, plain-transaction-light, and fast. The model's McFadden
pseudo-R² is 0.12 and the VIFs below confirm the three
signals are not proxies for one another.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
vif(m1) %>% knitr::kable(col.names = "VIF",
                         caption = "Variance inflation factors — the three behavioural signals carry distinct information.")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Variance inflation factors — the three behavioural signals carry distinct information.

|           |      VIF|
|:----------|--------:|
|tnx_log    | 2.183933|
|erc20_log  | 2.320851|
|avgmin_log | 1.332773|


:::
:::


## From scores to decisions

A logistic model outputs probabilities; a fraud screen must output decisions, and
the gap between those two is where the operational honesty lives.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
roc1 <- roc(lr$flag, fitted(m1), quiet = TRUE)
plot(roc1, legacy.axes = TRUE, col = "#2C7FB8",
     main = sprintf("Behaviour-only model — AUC %.2f", auc(roc1)))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Model 1 discrimination. AUC summarises ranking quality across all possible thresholds — it says nothing about any particular operating point.](05-fraud-logr_files/figure-html/m1-roc-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pred1 <- factor(ifelse(fitted(m1) >= 0.5, "Fraudulent", "Legitimate"),
                levels = c("Legitimate", "Fraudulent"))
cm <- table(pred1, lr$flag_f)

data.frame(
  ` ` = c("Predicted: Legitimate", "Predicted: Fraudulent"),
  `Actual: Legitimate` = cm[, "Legitimate"],
  `Actual: Fraudulent` = cm[, "Fraudulent"],
  check.names = FALSE
) %>%
  knitr::kable(row.names = FALSE,
               caption = "Confusion matrix at the default 0.5 threshold.")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Confusion matrix at the default 0.5 threshold.

|                      | Actual: Legitimate| Actual: Fraudulent|
|:---------------------|------------------:|------------------:|
|Predicted: Legitimate |               7594|               1085|
|Predicted: Fraudulent |                 68|                265|


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
tibble(
  metric = c("AUC", "Accuracy", "Sensitivity (fraud recall)", "Specificity"),
  value = c(as.numeric(auc(roc1)),
            sum(diag(cm)) / sum(cm),
            cm["Fraudulent", "Fraudulent"] / sum(cm[, "Fraudulent"]),
            cm["Legitimate", "Legitimate"] / sum(cm[, "Legitimate"]))
) %>% knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Operating characteristics at the 0.5 threshold.")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Operating characteristics at the 0.5 threshold.

|metric                     | value|
|:--------------------------|-----:|
|AUC                        | 0.711|
|Accuracy                   | 0.872|
|Sensitivity (fraud recall) | 0.196|
|Specificity                | 0.991|


:::
:::


The matrix tells the story the accuracy number hides. At the default threshold the
model catches 265 of the
1350 flagged addresses
(sensitivity 20%)
while raising only 68 false alarms — and still
posts 87% accuracy, because with
85% of addresses legitimate, accuracy mostly rewards saying "legitimate". The 0.5
threshold is a statistical default, not a policy: in a real fraud operation the
threshold is set by the relative cost of a missed fraud versus an investigation,
and lowering it here would trade a manageable rise in false alarms for a large
gain in recall. The behavioural signals rank addresses usefully
(AUC 0.71); what to *do* with the ranking is a
business decision.

## Model 2: does the token ecosystem change what behaviour means?

Chapter 3 found the dominant received token *associated* with fraud; the sharper
question is whether it *moderates* the behavioural signals — whether the same
pattern of activity means something different inside different token communities.
Model 2 interacts all three behavioural predictors with the token factor.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
m2 <- glm(flag ~ (tnx_log + erc20_log + avgmin_log) * token_rec,
          family = binomial, data = lr)

# Wald intervals: several token × behaviour cells contain (almost) only one
# class, so profile-likelihood CIs do not converge for those terms.
tidy(m2) %>%
  mutate(OR = exp(estimate),
         conf.low = exp(estimate - 1.96 * std.error),
         conf.high = exp(estimate + 1.96 * std.error)) %>%
  select(term, OR, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) %>%
  filter(p.value < 0.05) %>%
  arrange(p.value) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Model 2 — significant terms only (Wald 95% CIs; full table in the folded code).")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Model 2 — significant terms only (Wald 95% CIs; full table in the folded code).

|term                                           |           OR|  conf.low|    conf.high| p.value|
|:----------------------------------------------|------------:|---------:|------------:|-------:|
|token_recLow Count                             | 1.459604e+03|   506.676| 4.204747e+03|   0.000|
|(Intercept)                                    | 3.000000e-03|     0.001| 9.000000e-03|   0.000|
|token_recLivepeer Token                        | 7.562441e+05| 36117.485| 1.583458e+07|   0.000|
|erc20_log:token_recLivepeer Token              | 0.000000e+00|     0.000| 0.000000e+00|   0.000|
|token_recXENON                                 | 3.364772e+03|   344.826| 3.283303e+04|   0.000|
|token_recTronix                                | 6.183820e+02|    80.319| 4.760964e+03|   0.000|
|erc20_log:token_recXENON                       | 1.000000e-03|     0.000| 8.000000e-03|   0.000|
|erc20_log:token_recLow Count                   | 1.800000e-02|     0.004| 7.600000e-02|   0.000|
|token_recDATAcoin                              | 1.891263e+04|   523.198| 6.836568e+05|   0.000|
|token_recblockwell.ai KYC Casper Token         | 1.965601e+05|  1397.492| 2.764658e+07|   0.000|
|erc20_log                                      | 3.083700e+01|     7.436| 1.278870e+02|   0.000|
|token_recINS Promo                             | 5.086892e+04|   350.530| 7.382106e+06|   0.000|
|erc20_log:token_recDATAcoin                    | 0.000000e+00|     0.000| 0.000000e+00|   0.000|
|tnx_log:token_recblockwell.ai KYC Casper Token | 3.700000e-02|     0.007| 1.990000e-01|   0.000|
|tnx_log:token_recVIU                           | 2.300000e-02|     0.002| 2.680000e-01|   0.003|
|tnx_log:token_recLivepeer Token                | 1.430000e-01|     0.039| 5.230000e-01|   0.003|
|token_recVIU                                   | 5.249525e+03|    15.544| 1.772910e+06|   0.004|
|tnx_log:token_recTronix                        | 2.390000e-01|     0.083| 6.850000e-01|   0.008|
|tnx_log:token_recLow Count                     | 4.080000e-01|     0.210| 7.910000e-01|   0.008|
|avgmin_log:token_recDATAcoin                   | 2.386000e+00|     1.215| 4.685000e+00|   0.012|
|erc20_log:token_recTronix                      | 1.060000e-01|     0.018| 6.370000e-01|   0.014|
|token_recKickCoin                              | 6.731249e+18|  5478.739| 8.270099e+33|   0.014|
|token_recEOS                                   | 9.960050e+04|     9.085| 1.091914e+09|   0.015|
|tnx_log:token_recINS Promo                     | 3.000000e-03|     0.000| 3.340000e-01|   0.016|
|avgmin_log:token_recKickCoin                   | 0.000000e+00|     0.000| 3.310000e-01|   0.024|
|tnx_log:token_recKickCoin                      | 0.000000e+00|     0.000| 2.370000e-01|   0.026|
|tnx_log:token_recOmiseGO                       | 1.840000e-01|     0.034| 9.980000e-01|   0.050|


:::
:::


The table already hints at what is happening: token-level odds ratios in the
hundreds of thousands, interaction ORs indistinguishable from zero. Estimates that
extreme are the signature of near-empty cells — token groups where one class
barely occurs — pushing coefficients toward the boundary. They are shown for
completeness, not interpreted individually; the trustworthy comparison is at the
model level.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
lrt <- anova(m1, m2, test = "Chisq")
roc2 <- roc(lr$flag, fitted(m2), quiet = TRUE)

tibble(model = c("M1: behaviour only", "M2: + token-type interactions"),
       pseudo_R2 = c(mcfadden(m1), mcfadden(m2)),
       AUC = c(as.numeric(auc(roc1)), as.numeric(auc(roc2))),
       AIC = c(AIC(m1), AIC(m2))) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Model comparison. The likelihood-ratio test for the added terms is decisive (p < .001) — the interesting question is why.")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


Table: Model comparison. The likelihood-ratio test for the added terms is decisive (p < .001) — the interesting question is why.

|model                         | pseudo_R2|   AUC|      AIC|
|:-----------------------------|---------:|-----:|--------:|
|M1: behaviour only            |     0.117| 0.711| 6727.439|
|M2: + token-type interactions |     0.753| 0.983| 2023.764|


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(roc1, col = "#2C7FB8", legacy.axes = TRUE,
     main = "Adding token context: dramatic — and suspicious")
plot(roc2, col = "#D95F02", add = TRUE)
legend("bottomright",
       legend = c(sprintf("M1: behaviour only (AUC %.2f)", auc(roc1)),
                  sprintf("M2: + token context (AUC %.2f)", auc(roc2))),
       col = c("#2C7FB8", "#D95F02"), lwd = 2, bty = "n")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Both models' ROC curves. The jump is real in-sample — and the next paragraphs explain why it should not be taken at face value.](05-fraud-logr_files/figure-html/roc-both-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::




### Reading the jump carefully

An AUC of 0.98 from a logistic regression should
raise an eyebrow before it raises applause, and here the skepticism pays off. Part
of the improvement is genuine segment structure — Chapter 3's association result
made that expected. But a decisive part of it is simpler:
**2 token levels in this sample coincide perfectly with the fraud
flag** — *Blockwell say NOTSAFU* (n = 779) and *GSENetwork* (n = 80)
are flagged fraudulent without exception. Both are known spam-airdrop tokens, and
the likely mechanism runs through how the dataset was built: the fraud list appears
to draw heavily on addresses swept up in those scam ecosystems, so receiving these
tokens is partly a *consequence* of how the label was constructed rather than an
independent behavioural signal. Combined with the missingness artefact found
earlier, the message is consistent: this dataset rewards models that memorise its
construction.

For anyone taking this analysis toward a production model, three practices follow
naturally: validate out-of-time rather than in-sample, so construction artefacts
stop paying; ablate token *identity* and check how much discrimination survives on
behaviour alone (here, an AUC of 0.71); and treat
token context as segment information whose meaning must be re-learned as scam
ecosystems change, not as a stable feature. Read that way, Model 2 is best
understood as strong evidence that token context *carries* signal — and a worked
example of why a fraud analyst never deploys an in-sample 0.98.

## What this chapter established

Behaviour alone ranks addresses usefully — token-heavy, plain-transaction-light,
fast-cadence profiles carry materially higher flag odds — and token context adds
real but contaminated lift whose honest assessment requires validation this
cross-sectional dataset cannot provide. The estimates speak for token-active
addresses, against a label of undocumented provenance, in-sample only; the
deployment-grade treatment of this problem — held-out protocols, imbalance
handling, cost-sensitive thresholds — is the subject of the dedicated
machine-learning project that this suite's conclusions point toward.
